Literal: namāmī = I bow; īśam = Lord; īśāna = Īśāna (the highest form of Śiv); nirvāṇa = mokṣa (liberation); rūpaṃ = form.
Meaning1: I bow to Lord Īśāna, whose form is Nirvāṇa.
Meaning2: I bow to Lord Īśāna, the embodiment of blissful liberation.

Literal: vibhum - the all-pervading; vyāpakam - the omnipresent; brahma - Brahman (absolute-reality); veda - knowledge; svarūpam - one's own form.
Meaning1: The all-pervading, the omnipresent, whose own form is the knowledge of Brahman.
Meaning2: He is all-pervading and omnipresent, whose self-essence is the knowledge of the absolute.

Literal: nijam - one's own, inherent, eternal; nirguṇam - beyond the Guṇas (the three modes of nature); nirvikalpam - without duality or distinction; nirīham - without desire or effort.
Meaning1: The inherent, beyond the Guṇas, without distinction or duality, without movement due to desire or effort.
Meaning2: He who eternally dwells absorbed within Himself, beyond all qualities and attributes, immutable, still, without desire or effort.

Literal: cit-ākāśam - the infinite consciousness (cit = consciousness; ākāśa = space or sky); ākāśa-vāsam - the dweller in the sky (or space); bhaje - I worship; aham - I.
Meaning1: I worship the Cidākāśa, the dweller in the Ākāśa.
Meaning2: I worship the absolute consciousness pervading all space.

Literal: nirākāram - without form, formless; oṃkāra - the Oṃ/Praṇava (the primordial sound from which all vibration arises); mūlam - root; turīyam - the fourth state of consciousness (the transcendental state beyond waking, dreaming, and deep sleep).
Meaning1: Formless, the root of the Oṃkāra, the Turīya.
Meaning2: He is without form, the root from which the primordial sound arises, ever abiding as the transcendental state of consciousness.
